1:
"And Solomon the son of David was strengthened in his kingdom." The witnesses open the book by weighing that word "strengthened," and they read the young reign as the work of God before it is the work of Solomon. The reformed John Gill takes it of a throne now well settled and established, the king secured on his father's seat after the removal of certain men from whom he might have looked for trouble. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry presses the same note into a doctrine: though Solomon came to a contested title, God being with him he was strengthened in his kingdom — his heart and his hands strengthened, and his interest in the people confirmed — so that Henry draws the maxim, "God's presence will be our strength."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the Hebrew: not merely that Solomon was strengthened, but that he "showed himself strong over his kingdom," firmly grasping the reins of power and proving a strong ruler, a sense he compares with the notices of later reigns at 2 Chronicles 17:1. He observes too what the chronicler leaves out: all the palace intrigue of the accession told in 1 Kings 1-2 falls away as outside his purpose, and the writer takes up his story where the older history had ended, with the kingdom "established in the hand of Solomon" at 1 Kings 2:46. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke makes the same point of continuity — the very opening shows the book to be a continuation of the preceding one, not a fresh start — and lingers on the clause "the LORD his God was with him," which the Targum renders, strikingly, "the Word of the Lord was his support."

The witnesses gather most warmly around that clause and its climax, "and magnified him exceedingly." Ellicott hears in "magnified" the making good of what had been promised the house of David, comparing 1 Chronicles 29:25. Henry will not let the reader forget that this greatness was at once contested and given: the strengthening was not Solomon's achievement but heaven's gift, so that the throne rests from the first upon the presence of God. Clarke's Targum touches the same nerve from the side of the divine word — it is the LORD's own "support" that holds the king up. So the schools do not divide. The grammatical-historical reading treats the verse as the chronicler's deliberate frame, suppressing the struggles to open on the strength; the puritan reading treats it as his theology, first the presence and then the prosperity. Together they set the note the whole chapter will sound: a king made strong not by cunning or by conquest but by the God who was with him, whose being "with" Solomon is the ground of every gift — the wisdom, the riches, the honour — yet to be asked and given.
2:
The first act of the strengthened king is not a decree but a summons to worship, and the witnesses attend to whom he gathers and why. The reformed John Gill reads the verse of Solomon addressing the representatives of the nation about going up to Gibeon to sacrifice, and notes that Jarchi and Kimchi understood it so. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees that he "spake" concerning his intention of going to Gibeon, and that the great men were to attend him thither; Adam Clarke adds a chronological note, that this is supposed to have fallen in the second year of the reign.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the grammar. The Hebrew, he says, is better rendered "commanded all Israel" — the Vulgate's praecepit — and the long roll that follows, "the captains of thousands and of hundreds, and the judges, and every governor, the chief of the fathers," is not a second group but an apposition explaining what "all Israel" means: the national representatives. He remarks that the account in the older history allows only a single verse for the whole sacrifice at 1 Kings 3:4 and so omits to mention that the princes took part, whereas the chronicler, true to his manner in the great assemblies gathered under David at 1 Chronicles 13:1, brings the leaders forward. "Every governor," Ellicott explains, is the Hebrew nasi, the prince or emir of a tribe, and "the chief of the fathers" defines it further as the heads of the clans.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the muster of officers into a lesson on public religion. All Solomon's great men, he says, must thus far be good men, that they should join with him in worshipping God; the king spoke to the captains and judges, the governors and chief of the fathers, to go with him to Gibeon, and authority and interest are well bestowed on those who will so use them for the glory of God and the promoting of religion. It is our duty, Henry urges, to engage all with whom we have any influence in the solemnities of religion — the more that join with us the better, for it is the more like heaven. And he marks the timing as an omen: Solomon began his reign with this public, pious visit to God's altar, and magistrates are then likely to do well for themselves and their people when they thus take God along with them at their setting out. The witnesses do not contend. Gill and Ellicott fix who the assembly was, Wesley and Clarke when and why it moved, and Henry what it teaches — that a kingdom rightly begun is a kingdom brought, princes and people together, to the altar of God before ever it turns to the business of the throne.
3:
Where the assembly goes, and why it should go there rather than to Jerusalem, is the question the witnesses take up as the congregation moves toward Gibeon. The reformed John Gill notes simply that all the congregation agreed to Solomon's motion and went up with him to the high place. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott supplies the reason the verse itself gives, and dwells on the phrase "the tabernacle of the congregation of God," which he would render more exactly "God's tent of meeting" — the place of meeting, that is, with man, in the sense of Exodus 25:22. Solomon repaired to Gibeon, Ellicott observes, because that was "the great high place" of 1 Kings 3:4; and the chronicler's own words tell us why it stood pre-eminent above every other high place: the Mosaic tent of meeting was there, as the fuller notices of the Levitical service at 1 Chronicles 16:39 confirm.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the journey against the piety of the previous reign, and refuses to make a quarrel of the difference. David his father, Henry says, was a prophet and a psalmist and kept mostly to the ark, which he had brought up to Zion; but Solomon, having read much in his Bible concerning the tabernacle that Moses built and the altars that stood there, paid more respect to them than, it should seem, David had done. Both did well, Henry insists, and let neither be censured. If the zeal of one good man be carried out most to one instance of religion, and of another to some other instance, let them not judge nor despise one another. The son's turning to the ancient sanctuary at Gibeon is no reproach to the father's devotion at the ark; each honoured God according to the light and occasion given him.

So the witnesses converge on a single portrait of the place and the act. Ellicott establishes the historical fact — that Gibeon held the tent Moses made, and was therefore the legitimate great high place — while Henry draws out the devotion, that a wise king would begin his reign at the altar his fathers had honoured. Together they let the verse do its quiet work: the whole nation, led by its new king, going up in one body to the tent of meeting, so that the government to come may be founded on an act of corporate worship at the very sanctuary where God had promised to meet with His people. The high place at Gibeon is not, in their reading, a rival to Zion but the appointed ground on which Solomon seeks the God who will presently appear to him there.
4:
The verse pauses to explain a divided sanctuary — the ark in one city, the tabernacle and altar in another — and the witnesses take pains to trace how Israel's holy things came to be so scattered. The wesleyan-arminian voices set out the plain history. Adam Clarke explains that the tabernacle and the brazen altar still remained at Gibeon, but that David had brought away the ark out of the tabernacle and placed it in a tent at Jerusalem, pointing back to the removal recorded at 2 Samuel 6:2. John Wesley reads the same fact with an eye to David's intention: he separated the ark from the tabernacle and brought it up to Jerusalem, meaning there to build for it a more noble and lasting habitation than the old tent could be — the temple his son would raise.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the connecting word and the wording of the transfer. The "but" that opens the verse he would render "but indeed" or "but no doubt," a particle marking the contrast between the altar left at Gibeon and the ark carried away; and for the whole history of that removal he refers the reader to the narratives at 1 Chronicles 13 and the parallel in 2 Samuel 6:17. The clause "to the place which David had prepared" he takes closely — "into that which David had prepared for it" — and the word "pitched" he renders "spread," the same term used of the tent David spread for the ark at 1 Chronicles 15:1. The reformed John Gill passes the verse quickly, content to point the reader back to the account of the ark's coming up from Kirjath-jearim.

The witnesses do not divide, and the effect of their agreement is to explain the strange geography of Solomon's worship. The ark, the throne of the divine presence, already stood in a tent at Jerusalem, brought there by David in hope of the house to come; the brazen altar, the appointed place of sacrifice, still stood before the Mosaic tabernacle at Gibeon. A godly king who wished to sacrifice lawfully had therefore to go where the altar was, even while the ark rested elsewhere. Clarke and Wesley fix the fact and David's purpose behind it; Ellicott secures the language and the cross-references; Gill defers to the history already told. Between them they show that the split was no irregularity of Solomon's making but the inherited arrangement of his father's reign — an arrangement that made Gibeon the only proper resort for the great sacrifice of the previous verses, and that the temple, when built, would at last heal, gathering ark and altar under one roof.
5:
The chronicler singles out the brazen altar and names its ancient maker, and the witnesses read the notice as his careful defence of why Gibeon, and not Zion, was the place of sacrifice. The reformed John Gill fixes the altar's pedigree: Bezaleel the son of Uri had made it according to the pattern given by Moses at the direction of God, so that this was no local shrine but the very altar of the wilderness tabernacle. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the closing clause devotionally — that Solomon and the congregation "sought" the LORD and His favour by hearty prayers and sacrifices in the place which God Himself had appointed.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott brings the philologist's caution to a disputed word. The Hebrew consonants may be pointed either sham, "there," or sam, "he put"; some manuscripts, with the Septuagint and Vulgate, read the former, while most, with the Syriac, Arabic, and Targum, read the latter. Ellicott judges "there" the preferable reading, reasoning that it is not likely David had found the brazen altar separated from the Mosaic sanctuary and then restored it to its place; better to say simply that the altar "was there" before the dwelling of Jehovah. That Bezaleel had made it he confirms from Exodus 38:1. And the last clause, "Solomon and the congregation sought unto it," he would render rather "sought Him" — that is, the LORD — the object being God and not the altar.

What binds the witnesses is the point Ellicott draws from the whole verse: the presence of the old brazen altar of Moses was precisely what constituted Gibeon the legitimate place of sacrifice. With this the chronicler is perfectly consistent, for he had already accounted, in the narrative of the threshing-floor at 1 Chronicles 21:28, for why David himself did not go up to Gibeon to inquire, being kept back by the sword of the angel. The verse, then, is no antiquarian aside but a piece of the chronicler's argument. Gill grounds the altar in the command of God to Moses; Wesley reads the seeking as true worship in the appointed place; Ellicott secures the text and its logic. Together they show that Solomon's thousand offerings were laid on no improvised altar but on the divinely-patterned altar of the fathers, at the one high place where the tent of meeting and the sacrificial hearth still stood — so that the king's great sacrifice, for all its being offered away from the ark, was offered exactly where the law and the history had appointed that it should be.
6:
Now the sacrifice itself is told, and the witnesses measure both the language of the offering and its sheer abundance. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott corrects the rendering of the opening: not that Solomon "went up thither to the brasen altar," as the Vulgate has it, but rather, with the Septuagint and Syriac, that he "offered there on the brasen altar." The phrase "before the LORD," he explains, means before the entry of the LORD's dwelling-place, where the altar stood according to Exodus 40:6; and the clause "which was at the tabernacle of the congregation" he takes to mean that this altar belonged to the tent of meeting, just as the golden altar is said in like manner to belong to the inner sanctuary at 1 Kings 6:22. He notes even the small stylistic touch that the verb "offered" is repeated before its object for the sake of clearness. The reformed John Gill adds only that Solomon "went up thither," up to the high place at Gibeon.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry supplies the largeness the bare verse implies. Solomon offered abundance of sacrifices to God there — a thousand burnt-offerings, and perhaps a far greater number of peace-offerings, on which he and his company feasted before the LORD. Where God sows plentifully, Henry observes, He expects to reap accordingly; and Solomon's father David had left him flocks and herds in abundance, as the roll of the royal officers at 1 Chronicles 27:29 records, so that the king was but giving God His dues out of what God had given him. Henry draws the geography into the theology: the ark was at Jerusalem, as v.4 had said, but the altar was at Gibeon, as v.5 had shown, and thither Solomon brought his sacrifices — "for it is the altar that sanctifieth every gift."

The witnesses do not divide; they simply divide the labour. Ellicott secures the exact sense — Solomon offered upon the altar before the entrance of the sacred tent — while Henry unfolds the greatness of the act and its lesson. The thousand victims are not extravagance for its own sake but the overflow of a heart that would begin its reign by rendering to God, and in abundance, out of the abundance God had bestowed. A king who has just been strengthened and magnified answers the gift with a royal thank-offering, and does so at the appointed altar, so that his liberality is at once devout and lawful. It is upon this night of sacrifice, the witnesses agree, that the great vision of the following verses will break.
7:
The night after the sacrifice brings the theophany, and the witnesses fasten on both its timing and its searching question. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke marks the moment precisely — it was the night following the sacrifice — and, rather than repeat what he had written elsewhere, refers the reader to his fuller treatment of Solomon's choice at 1 Kings 3:5. The reformed John Gill likewise observes that from this point to the end of the story the account runs the same as the older narrative, and is content to let the parallel carry the weight.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott compares the two records closely. The parallel says, "In Gibeon did Jehovah appear unto Solomon in a dream of the night," whereas the chronicler fixes the night as the one that followed the sacrifices, and it is the older text that explicitly makes the appearance a dream. The divine word Ellicott would render, more pointedly than the common version, "Ask thou! what shall I give thee?" — a summons flung open to the king's own desire, God offering to give whatever Solomon will name.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes that open offer the heart of the scene. God bade him ask what he would, Henry says, not only to put him in the right way of obtaining the favours intended for him — "ask, and you shall receive, that your joy may be full" — but also to try him, to discover what was in his heart. For men's characters appear in their choices and desires; the question "What wouldst thou have?" tries a man as much as the question "What wouldst thou do?" As we choose, Henry adds, so we shall have, and that is likely to be our portion to which we give the preference, whether the wealth and pleasure of this world or the riches and delights of the spirit. Thus God, by throwing the choice open, was proving whether Solomon was one of the children of this world, who say "Who will show us any good?" or one of the children of light, who say "Lord, lift up the light of thy countenance upon us."

The witnesses do not divide. Clarke and Gill anchor the vision in its parallel and its timing; Ellicott renders the challenge in its full openness; Henry reads it as a test of the heart. Between them they turn a single verse into a probe of every reader: the God who appears by night and says "Ask what I shall give thee" is offering not merely a gift but a mirror, in which the true bent of the soul — toward earth or toward heaven — is made to show itself in what it dares to ask.
8:
Solomon's answer opens not with a request but with a confession of grace received, and the witnesses weigh the humility with which he approaches the throne of God. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the first words emphatically — "Thou, thou hast done great kindness with David my father" — and observes that from this point the chronicler's relation is on the whole briefer than the older one, the greater part of the long verse at 1 Kings 3:6 being omitted. The clause "and hast made me to reign in his stead" he compares with 1 Kings 3:7, and, in a striking parallel from the monuments, with the language of Esarhaddon king of Assyria, who ascribed his secure seat upon his father's throne to the gods who set him there.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the prayer as the ground of the divine promise to follow, noting how the chronicler's mode of expression is throughout briefer than at 1 Kings 3:11 and following, and that the reckoning of God's kindness rests, as the word suggests, on treasure freely given, a term used of the spoils divided in Joshua 22:8. But the deepest note here is sounded by the two witnesses who read the verse as a lesson in filial dependence. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley paraphrases Solomon's heart: "Give me the spirit of my father David, that Israel may not suffer by the change"; and he draws the moral that the eminency of those who went before us, and the obligation that lies upon us to keep up and carry on the good work they were engaged in, should quicken our prayers for wisdom and grace, that we may do the work of God in our day as faithfully as they did in theirs.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry says the very same thing in his own key. "Thou hast made me reign in my father's stead": Lord, Henry hears the king reason, Thou hast put me into this place, and therefore I can in faith ask of Thee grace to do the duty of it — for what service we have reason to believe God calls us to, we have reason to hope He will qualify us for. And more: Thou hast put me into this place in the stead of David, the great and good man who filled it up so well; therefore give me wisdom, that Israel may not suffer damage by the change. "Must I reign in my father's stead? Lord, give me my father's spirit." So the witnesses close ranks. Ellicott and Keil secure the text and its brevity; Wesley and Henry, from their kindred school, hear in Solomon's opening words the prayer of every successor who knows that the place is God's gift and the grace to fill it must be God's gift too.
9:
From gratitude Solomon turns to a plea, and it is a plea built upon a promise — "let thy promise unto David my father be established" — which the witnesses read as the truest kind of prayer. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears in the words a reminiscence of David's own prayer at 1 Chronicles 17:23, "let the thing that thou hast spoken be established for ever." The clause "over a people like the dust of the earth in multitude" he renders "a people numerous as the dust of the earth," noting that it corresponds freely with the older text at 1 Kings 3:8, and comparing the standing boast of the Assyrian monarchs who styled themselves "king of multitudes."

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lingers, as he loves to do, over a single word and the Targum's rendering of it. "Let thy promise" is in the Hebrew "thy word," which the Targumist gives as pithgam; and Clarke marks a curious and consistent usage — that when either God or man is represented as having spoken a word, the paraphrast uses pithgam, but when "word" is used personally, as an agent, he employs instead meymera, "the Word," which Clarke takes to answer to the divine Logos of John 1:1. It is a philological aside that opens, in his hands, onto the whole mystery of the Word of the LORD.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws out the theology of praying by promise. Solomon means the promise concerning his successor, the covenant word given to David at 2 Samuel 7:13 — "he shall build a house for my name, I will establish his throne." Wisdom itself was never named in that promise, Henry grants, yet it was necessary to its accomplishment; and so Solomon pleads the promise as the ground of his petition. From this Henry gathers two rules. First, God's promises are our best pleas in prayer: "Remember thy word unto thy servant." Second, children may take the comfort of the promises of that covenant which their parents, in dedicating them, laid claim to and took hold of on their behalf. So the witnesses, each after his manner, honour the same movement of faith. Ellicott finds it echoing David's prayer; Clarke finds in its very word a hint of the personal Word; Henry finds in it the pattern of all believing petition — to bring God's own promise back to Him, and to ask for the wisdom without which that promise cannot be made good.
10:
Here at last comes the petition itself — "Give me now wisdom and knowledge, that I may go out and come in before this people" — and the witnesses admire both the choice and the exact words in which it is framed. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry celebrates the choice as worthy of David's son: like a genuine child of his father, Solomon chose spiritual blessings rather than temporal. His petition is "Give me wisdom and knowledge"; he owns these to be desirable gifts and God to be the giver of them, as Proverbs 2:6 teaches that the LORD gives wisdom. God gave the faculty of understanding, Henry adds, and to Him we must apply for the furnishing of it — the young king asking not for the trappings of power but for the mind to use it well.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the philology beside the piety. The petition, he says, is co-ordinate with the plea of the previous verse — "Now, O LORD God... now, wisdom and knowledge give thou me" — and it answers to the request at 1 Kings 3:9. The word rendered "knowledge," madda, is a late one, occurring besides only in Daniel 1:4 and at Ecclesiastes 10:20 — a small mark of the chronicler's later Hebrew. The phrase "go out and come in before this people" he traces to the old idiom for leadership at Numbers 27:17 and Deuteronomy 31:2; and the closing cry, "who can judge this thy people, that is so great?", he sets against the parallel's "who is able to judge?", noting that the chronicler calls the people "great" where the older text calls them "numerous," and that the king's first office is here conceived as that of a judge, in the sense of 1 Samuel 8:20. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm the general observation that the chronicler's phrasing throughout this speech is briefer than that of the older account.

The witnesses converge without a jar. Henry weighs the heart of the request — that Solomon asked for the best thing, understanding to govern; Ellicott weighs its words — the late term for knowledge, the ancient idiom for leadership, the king conceived as judge over a people too great to govern by wit alone. Between them they show why the request was so pleasing to God: the man to whom any gift was offered asked for the one gift that would fit him to serve the rest, wisdom and knowledge to go out and come in before a people no unaided prudence could ever number or judge.
11:
God's reply begins by rehearsing what Solomon did not ask, and the witnesses find the whole worth of the request measured in that catalogue of things declined. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the wording of the divine commendation. The phrase "because this was in thine heart" he compares with 1 Chronicles 22:7; and he notes that "wealth" among the things not sought is the chronicler's own addition, the late word nekasim, common in the Targums and in Syriac, the same triad "riches, wealth, and honour" recurring at Ecclesiastes 6:2. "Long life" he glosses as "many days." The closing clause — "but hast asked wisdom and knowledge for thyself, that thou mayest judge my people" — he calls an expansion of the older text's briefer "hast asked for thyself discernment to hear judgment" at 1 Kings 3:11, remarking that the very verb "hast asked" is here cast in better idiom than in the parallel.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as the opening of the divine promise, and note again that the chronicler's mode of expression is briefer than in the older record, so that the conditional promise of long life which the parallel attaches is here left out. What God fastens on, in their reading, is the disposition of the heart: the request is honoured because of what it reveals of the man who made it.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the commendation into a doctrine of answered prayer. God gave Solomon the wisdom he asked for, Henry says, precisely because he asked for it — for wisdom is a gift that God bestows as freely and liberally as any, upon those who value it and wrestle for it and resolve to use it, and He "upbraideth not," as James 1:5 promises to all who lack wisdom and ask of God. God's grace, Henry concludes, shall never be wanting to those who sincerely desire to know and to do their duty. So the witnesses stand together. Ellicott and Keil trace how the chronicler shapes and shortens the divine speech, marking his later diction and his omissions; Henry hears in that speech the settled principle of the kingdom of God, that the LORD delights to grant the very wisdom His servants ask, and grants it the more freely because it was asked in preference to every lower thing.
12:
The grant itself now falls, and it exceeds the asking — wisdom given, and with it the riches and honour Solomon had passed by — so that the witnesses read the verse as the great proof that God outgives His petitioners. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott handles the wording with care. "Wisdom and knowledge" he renders "the wisdom and the knowledge," namely those the king had asked for; the term "is granted unto thee" is a rare Hebrew expression found besides only at Esther 3:11. Where the older account spreads the promise over three verses at 1 Kings 3:12 and following, the chronicler compresses it into one; his "I will give thee" answers the parallel's "I have given." The promise "such as none of the kings have had... neither shall there any after thee have the like" Ellicott renders "such as hath not been to the kings before thee, and after thee shall not be," comparing the like superlative at 1 Chronicles 29:25 and remarking how the Assyrian kings loved just such comparisons of themselves with all who went before. He notes too that the conditional promise of long life, given in the older text, is here omitted — perhaps, he suggests, because Solomon fell short of it — though the covenant assurance is renewed to him later at 2 Chronicles 7:17.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch see in this grant the hinge of the chapter's design: to prove by facts the fulfilment of the divine promise, the older history goes on to tell of Solomon's wise judgment and his officers, but the chronicler turns instead to the plain evidences of the promised riches — the chariots, the horses, and the wealth that the closing verses will recount.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draw the same moral from the overflow. Henry marvels that God gave Solomon the wealth and honour he did not ask for, because he asked not for them: those who pursue present things most earnestly are the most likely to miss them, while those who refer themselves to the providence of God, if they have not the most of this world, have the most comfort in it. Wesley states the maxim in almost the same breath — those who make this world their end come short of the other, and frequently of this too; but those who make the other world their end shall obtain that, and shall have as much of this world as is convenient for them in their way. So the witnesses unite in wonder: the man who sought only wisdom is given wisdom and the world besides, a living proof that the kingdom sought first brings all these things added to it.
13:
With the vision ended, Solomon comes back from the high place to his capital and takes up his reign, and the witnesses divide their attention between a knot in the wording and the piety of the king's order of business. The reformed John Gill notes that the verse, read without the supplied words, may be taken as the Septuagint and Vulgate take it — Solomon came from the high place at Gibeon to Jerusalem. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the same correction: the Hebrew as it stands reads oddly, "came to the high place that was in Gibeon to Jerusalem," but clearly we should read "from the high place," with the Septuagint and Vulgate, a difficulty already felt by the Syriac version, which glossed the sense. The phrase "from before the tabernacle of the congregation" he ties back to v.3 and v.6.

Ellicott then defends a clause some had thought idle. The remark "and reigned over Israel" is by no means superfluous, he argues against Bertheau, for it naturally introduces the sketch of the reign that follows, carrying the reader on from God's promise to its fulfilment. He observes that the chronicler here passes over two things the older history keeps — the sacrifices Solomon offered before the ark on his return, at 1 Kings 3:15, and the famous judgment between the two mothers at 1 Kings 3:16 and following — and insists it is unreasonable to seek any ground for such omissions but the free and legitimate exercise of the compiler's own discretion. That he did not slight Mount Zion as a place of sacrifice, Ellicott adds, is plain from his own narrative of the threshing-floor altar at 1 Chronicles 21:18.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the verse for its order of devotion and duty. Solomon came from before the tabernacle, and reigned over Israel: he would not do any acts of government, Henry observes, till he had done his acts of devotion — first the tabernacle, and then the throne; first honour given to God, and then honour taken to himself. And when he had obtained wisdom from God, he did not bury his talent, nor give himself up to ease and pleasure, but ministered the gift he had received and minded his business: "he reigned over Israel." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the notice plainly as the return from Gibeon to Jerusalem that opens the reign. The witnesses thus knit the verse together: Gill and Ellicott mend the text so that Solomon returns from the high place, Ellicott defends the chronicler's freedom in what he keeps and drops, and Henry hears in the sequence the model of a life that seeks God first and governs afterward.
14:
The reign's outward glory is now sketched in horses and chariots, and here the witnesses genuinely part company over whether the splendour is a blessing to admire or a sin to lament. The grammatical-historical voices report it as the promised riches made visible. Keil and Delitzsch observe that these verses correspond almost word for word to the older account at 1 Kings 10:26, save that the chronicler uses a more expressive verb, "he laid them" in the chariot cities, and they read the whole inventory as the factual proof that God had kept His word to enrich the king. Charles Ellicott confirms the near-identity with 1 Kings 10:26, explains that "which he placed" and the parallel's "brought them into the chariot cities" differ only in the pointing of one word, and takes the chariots and horsemen as plainly military, the "chariot cities" lying probably to the south toward Egypt — perhaps including the Simeonite towns Beth-marcaboth, "house of chariots," and Hazar-susim, "court of horses," named at 1 Chronicles 4:31. Adam Clarke, for the numbers, simply refers the reader to his note at 1 Kings 4:26.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry breaks the descriptive calm with a sharp question. Solomon gathered chariots and horsemen: "Shall we praise him for this? We praise him not." For the king had been expressly forbidden to multiply horses at Deuteronomy 17:16; and Henry remarks that we never find Solomon's good father David in a chariot or on horseback — a mule was the highest he mounted. We should endeavour, Henry urges, to excel those who went before us in goodness rather than in grandeur.

So a real difference opens between the schools, not over the facts but over their worth. The grammatical-historical witnesses read the chariots as the chronicler seems to intend them, the fulfilment of the promised wealth and honour of v.12; the puritan reads them as the first quiet breach of the law that would one day undo the king. Henry does not deny the magnificence; he questions the magnificence, setting the Deuteronomic prohibition against the royal parade of cavalry. The witnesses leave the tension standing where the text leaves it — glory and warning together — and it is Henry's misgiving, as the next verses on the Egyptian trade will show, that the wesleyan witnesses will presently take up and press much harder.
15:
The chronicler heaps up hyperbole to convey Solomon's wealth — silver and gold like stones, cedar like the common sycomore — and the witnesses attend to the exuberance of the figure and the note of vanity that a preacher hears beneath it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fix a small but telling divergence from the parallel: where the older text at 1 Kings 10:27, and even the later notice at 2 Chronicles 9:27, speak only of silver made plentiful as stones, the chronicler here adds "and gold." This is very suitable, they argue, since the comparison "like stones" has reference to quantity, and Solomon had gathered not silver only but gold too in like abundance.

Charles Ellicott, from the same grammatical-historical school, confirms the reading and refines it. Silver, gold, stones, and cedar each carry the definite article in the Hebrew; the added "and gold" is absent from the parallel and from the later chapter, and the Syriac drops it here as well, yet the other versions keep it, and it makes a natural heightening of the hyperbole. The "sycomore trees that are in the vale" he glosses from 1 Chronicles 27:28, the lowland trees so common as to be worthless; and he records that the Syriac, reaching for another image of abundance, reads instead "as the sand which is on the seashore." Adam Clarke, characteristically, points the reader to his fuller treatment of the wealth at 1 Kings 10:27.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the very heaping-up into a caution. Solomon made silver and gold so plentiful at Jerusalem that he made them cheap and common; and here, Henry says, is the difference between earthly and heavenly treasure. The increase of gold lowers the value of it, so that the more men have the less each piece is worth; but the increase of grace advances its price, for the more men have of grace the more they prize it. "How much better, therefore," Henry exclaims, "is it to get wisdom than gold!" — reading the king's glittering abundance as a silent argument for the very wisdom he had asked before he was ever given the wealth. The witnesses do not contend. Keil and Ellicott establish the exact text and its swelling figures of speech; Henry weighs those figures on the scales of the sanctuary, and finds that the cheapness gold takes on by abundance only proves the surpassing worth of the wisdom that never so declines.
16:
The last two verses turn to Solomon's horse-trade with Egypt, and at once the witnesses divide over a single obscure Hebrew word. The traditional rendering, kept in the common version, has Solomon importing "horses and linen yarn"; and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, retaining it, refers the reader for the whole vexed question to his note at 1 Kings 10:28, where the subject is particularly examined. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry likewise reads it as linen and takes it for a lesson in enterprise: Solomon opened a trade with Egypt, importing horses and linen-yarn, and exported again to the neighbouring kings, no doubt with great advantage. It is the wisdom of princes, Henry says, to promote industry and encourage trade in their dominions; and he guesses that Solomon may have taken the hint of the linen-manufacture from his mother, who had painted the virtuous woman that "maketh fine linen, and selleth it" at Proverbs 31:24 — "in all labour there is profit."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott disputes the traditional reading root and branch. The word behind "linen yarn," he argues, is miqweh, meaning a gathering or collection, as of the waters at Genesis 1:10; the verse should tell how "the export of horses for Solomon was from Egypt, and a company of the king's merchants fetched a drove of horses at a price," the same term repeated in an artless play on words. The "linen yarn" of the common version he calls a mere guess, resting on the likeness of miqweh to the words for "rope" and "line" at Joshua 2:18, and on the known fact that much linen was made in Egypt. He notes that the Vulgate turned the word into a place-name, "from Coa," and the Septuagint in the parallel "from Tekoa," and the Syriac gave yet another city — variations that only prove the text was felt to be obscure.

So the debate is genuine and unresolved. Clarke and Henry read "linen yarn," a second Egyptian import beside the horses, and build from it a picture of Solomon the merchant-king; Ellicott reads no linen at all, but a company of traders fetching horses in droves at a fixed price. What none of the witnesses disputes is the fact at the verse's core — that Solomon's cavalry was mounted from Egypt, and that his agents made of the traffic a lucrative royal monopoly. Whether the second commodity was fine linen or only a further drove of horses, the trade itself stands; and it is that Egyptian traffic, as the closing verse will show, on which the sternest of the witnesses will fasten a solemn warning.
17:
The chapter closes on prices and profits — a chariot for six hundred shekels, a horse for a hundred and fifty, and Solomon's agents supplying the kings of the Hittites and of Syria — and the witnesses range from dry arithmetic to grave alarm. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply register the traffic: chariots and horses fetched up out of Egypt and passed on to all the kings of the Hittites and the Syrians by the hand of Solomon's merchants. Charles Ellicott, of the same school, refines the opening phrase — "they caused to come up and to come out" — against the older text's "there came up and came out a chariot from Egypt," and finds the rest of the verse identical in both. Adam Clarke reckons the money, taking the shekel at its highest rate and making the price of a horse about two-and-twenty pounds.

But it is Clarke, the wesleyan-arminian, who will not leave the verse as a note on commerce. Adopting Bishop Warburton's judgment, he reads this Egyptian horse-trade as the seed of Solomon's ruin. Moses had expressly forbidden the king to multiply horses at Deuteronomy 17:16, precisely because a body of cavalry could not be raised without sending down into Egypt, with which people God had forbidden dangerous commerce. When Solomon violated this and multiplied horses to excess, as 1 Kings 4:26 records, the fatal consequences followed in a chain: he married Pharaoh's daughter, the first fruit of the traffic, then took the strange wives of 1 Kings 11:1, who drew him first to build idol-temples for their use and then to worship at them himself. The original of all this mischief, Clarke insists, was the forbidden trade with Egypt for horses; and this "opprobrious commerce," in which the king even turned factor for neighbouring monarchs at v.17, was continued by his successors to their hurt — so that Isaiah at length denounces the whole traffic and foretells that to forsake it would be to forsake their idolatries, at Isaiah 31:1.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, who had read the same trade more mildly as the enterprise of a wise prince, still shares the sense that the horses of Egypt sit uneasily in a chapter of blessing. So the witnesses close the chapter on a double note. Keil, Ellicott, and the merchant's ledger tell what Solomon did; Clarke, with Warburton behind him, tells what it cost — that the very abundance which crowned the king's God-given wisdom carried within it, in this one forbidden traffic, the first thread of the apostasy that would in the end unravel him.
